title: Foundation-Laying Ceremonies in Haste and Half-Completed Flood Relief!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. Direct visual review identified a frozen title-layer regression: the source prints `அடிக்கல் நாட்டு விழாக்களும்`, not `அழுக்கல் நாட்டு விழாக்களும்`. The source's final page also supplies the OCR-missed date `7.3.2016`. Both are restored in this isolated reading copy, but the canonical chapter ID, folder, source file, and metadata remain unchanged pending a separate structural correction. A spurious partial duplicate of the title in the OCR body is omitted, and other scan-confirmed OCR joins are repaired.
paragraphs: The election code of conduct has come into force throughout Tamil Nadu. But before it did, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa laid foundation stones every day for every project that came to her mind. Although she knew perfectly well that the election was only two months away, the only Chief Minister who could hurriedly lay and publicise foundation stones for government projects in this fashion would have to be the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu!

The Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly concluded on 20 February 2016 after the reply to the debate on the interim budget statement. Even though the budget debate lasted for the extremely short period of only four days, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa continued her usual practice of announcing numerous schemes each day under Rule 110. After the Assembly was adjourned on 20 January, inauguration and foundation-laying ceremonies continued every day to create an appearance of activity before the outside world. The election code of conduct has now put a full stop to those video-conference spectacles.

On 25 February 2016, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa inaugurated houses constructed for uniformed personnel under the “Your Own Home” scheme. Do you know when this scheme was announced? On 3 May 2012, she announced in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly that 36,000 houses would be built for police personnel under the “Your Own Home” scheme, and that they could pay the price either in cash or monthly instalments according to their preference. Even after four years, Jayalalithaa has now opened only 2,673 of the promised 36,000 houses. It is said that even those who received them are being pressed to pay the money immediately.

On 27 February 2016, Jayalalithaa laid foundation stones for buildings to be constructed by the Public Works Department at an estimated Rs 120 crore and 48 lakh, and for PWD schemes estimated at Rs 626 crore, 93 lakh and 47 thousand.

On that same day at the Secretariat, she laid the foundation stone for a thermal power project to be established at Uppur in Ramanathapuram district at a cost of Rs 12,778 crore. The Uppur power station was announced in the budget statement on 4 August 2011. Jayalalithaa announced it at the beginning of her government and laid its foundation stone when that government was about to end. Its estimated cost was then Rs 9,600 crore. Because of the delay, the present cost is Rs 12,778 crore—an additional Rs 3,178 crore.

At the same event, she laid foundation stones for projects estimated at Rs 119 crore, 22 lakh and 33 thousand. Although Jayalalithaa participated on the 27th, the Information Department released the news only on 1 March 2016.
On 28 February 2016, for the first time she emerged from Fort St George and laid foundation stones for various projects worth Rs 193 crore and 26 lakh in the Dr Radhakrishnan Nagar constituency.
On 1 March 2016, she is said to have laid foundation stones for bridges and road works estimated at Rs 1,354 crore, 78 lakh and 70 thousand.

A private party had offered to donate twenty-five acres on Vangal Road for the Karur Medical College. The Municipal Council met at 5.30 p.m. on 29 February 2016. Before the meeting, AIADMK district secretary Vijayabaskar reportedly arrived and held secret discussions among the councillors.

Until then, it had been said that the government medical college would be built on Vangal Road. Why was the location suddenly changed, and why is the medical college now to be built on municipal land? Why was the foundation stone laid in such haste in the very circumstances in which the council passed a resolution to submit the proposal? It is said that the site on Vangal Road was selected when Senthil Balaji was Transport Minister, and that because he is no longer in office, the project was rolled up and shelved and a decision was made to establish the college at Sanapiratti. Is this report true?

Chief Minister Jayalalithaa also laid foundation stones for bridges, flyovers and railway overbridges worth Rs 1,354 crore, 78 lakh and 70 thousand under the Highways Department; projects worth Rs 119 crore under the Municipal Administration and Water Supply Department; works worth Rs 93 crore under the Information Technology Department; and green-house construction in town panchayats worth Rs 420 crore.

On 2 March 2016, *Dinamalar* wrote: “The Information Department does not release on a single day all the news releases concerning events inaugurated by the Chief Minister. It normally releases one or two, and issues the rest on days when the Chief Minister does not come to the Secretariat. As the date of the Assembly election is about to be announced, news of the inauguration of all the events was supplied to newspapers by the Information Department yesterday.” Those words provide a fine illustration of how the AIADMK government's Information Department functions.
Jayalalithaa also laid foundation stones for college and hospital buildings, estimated at Rs 458 crore and 92 lakh, for the new government medical colleges to be established in Karur and Pudukkottai.

Jayalalithaa laid the foundation stone for the Karur Medical College on 1 March 2016. But it was through a special meeting of the Karur Municipal Council that a resolution was passed allotting its site. During the 2014 demands for grants in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly, Jayalalithaa ordered that Karur Government Hospital be upgraded into a medical college admitting 150 students. In October last year, she granted administrative approval and financial sanction of Rs 229 crore and 46 lakh to open a new medical college in Karur. A private party then offered to donate twenty-five acres on Vangal Road. The Municipal Council met at 5.30 p.m. on 29 February 2016. Before the meeting, AIADMK district secretary Vijayabaskar reportedly arrived and held secret discussions among the councillors. At the special meeting that followed, the head of the medical college requested that 30.27 acres of municipal land in Sanapiratti village be provided for the hospital. A resolution was passed approving its transfer, on the basis of the government's decision, to the requisitioning Health Department without any loss to the municipality.

Until then, it had been said that the government medical college would be built on Vangal Road. Why was the location suddenly changed, and why is the medical college now to be built on municipal land? Why was the foundation stone laid in such haste in the very circumstances in which the council passed a resolution to submit the proposal? It is said that the site on Vangal Road was selected when Senthil Balaji was Transport Minister, and that because he is no longer in office, the project was rolled up and shelved and a decision was made to establish the college at Sanapiratti. Is this report true? Is that why its foundation stone was hurriedly laid on 1 March 2016, two years after the college was announced?

As the AIADMK government's term draws to an end, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has hurriedly begun and inaugurated numerous announcements and project works. Do you know the value of the projects for which she laid foundation stones in just five days? She laid foundation stones for projects worth Rs 15,192 crore, 68 lakh and 50 thousand. When will all these projects be implemented? Have funds for them been included in the budget statement? Has the Assembly approved them? Were they announced in passing merely to deceive the people?

Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has ordered a three-per-cent interest subsidy on interest payable to banks for loans taken by micro, small and medium enterprises affected by the heavy rains. She issued the order on 1 March 2016. The heavy rain fell at the end of November and beginning of December 2015. Why did she fail to issue this announcement for three months? Could it not have been included in the interim budget statement? Could it not have been announced when the opposition parties asked for an interest subsidy for rain-affected micro and small enterprises? After the interim budget statement, reports were read every day under Rule 110. Why was it not mentioned even then? Had it been announced at that time, the concession would have had to be given to those entitled to it. By announcing it now, can they not escape by replying, “The election notification has arrived; what can we do?”

This government has continually tormented every section of society and denied them a livelihood. Like the tyrant who told people asking for a piece of bread to “buy cake,” Jayalalithaa has staged foundation-laying ceremonies and made various announcements at the last moment—announcements that cannot be implemented and for which no time remains—merely to deceive the people. Udanpirappē, you must explain all these details to the public wherever you go and make them understand. The first task in election work is to make the people aware of the truth! I know well that you will carry out that work constructively. Even so, make these truths clear to the people along with the other facts!

Together with this announcement, Jayalalithaa made several more announcements on 1 March 2016 for micro, small and medium enterprises. Why this delay in making them? Did the departmental file take all this time merely to reach the Chief Minister for approval and signature?

Tamil Nadu endured enormous rainfall and surging floods, hundreds of lives were lost, and lakhs of families lost their possessions. Yet accusations are coming from every direction that the relief money has still not been paid in full. For example, today's *Dinamalar*, dated 6 March 2016, carries on its front page the headline, “No relief received: four lakh families lament,” with a photograph and report of flood victims from Chennai, Tiruvottiyur and Manali besieging the Tiruvottiyur taluk office in protest at not receiving relief. The report says Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced that the relief would be credited to everyone's bank account by 11 January, but that more than four lakh families had still not received it even after over two months. If this is the condition in Chennai itself, what must the condition be in the other affected districts?

It was announced that small traders and pavement vendors affected by the heavy rain would receive interest-free loans of Rs 5,000 from cooperative banks, and special camps were conducted. Reports now say that around Rs 100 crore in such loans was given only to members of the ruling party and that, as a result, the cooperative banks have reached the verge of bankruptcy.

There is no need even to explain the suffering of farmers. In an interview, Farmers' Association president Ayyakannu said: “Chief Minister Jayalalithaa refuses even to meet representatives of farmers. She also refuses to distribute the Rs 1,774 crore in drought relief supplied by the Central government to coconut, mango and lemon farmers. Condemning this conduct and demanding the waiver of agricultural loans, we announced a protest to besiege Jayalalithaa's residence on 1 March. We hoped she would at least then hold talks, but instead she unleashed repression against us. She treats us like terrorists. This government regards farmers as insignificant. This lady has made us, who feed the entire town, eat dirt.”
He spoke with a heart overcome by pain.

Thus, this government has continually tormented every section of society and denied them a livelihood. Like the tyrant who told people asking for a piece of bread to “buy cake,” Jayalalithaa has staged foundation-laying ceremonies and made various announcements at the last moment—announcements that cannot be implemented and for which no time remains—merely to deceive the people. Udanpirappē, you must explain all these details to the public wherever you go and make them understand. The first task in election work is to make the people aware of the truth! I know well that you will carry out that work constructively. Even so, make these truths clear to the people along with the other facts!
With affection,
M. K.
7 March 2016
